summary: An Upcountry Maui brush fire began overnight, eventually growing to about a thousand acres.
A smaller brush fire near a Lahaina school at 6:40am prompted early evacuations.
Fire officials declared the early Lahaina fire fully contained just before 9am.
NWS Honolulu issued red-flag and high-wind warnings at 9:26am as fire threat rose.
A Lahaina fire flareup at 3:30pm prompted fresh evacuations and a shelter-in-place advisory.
Lieutenant Governor Sylvia Luke signed an emergency proclamation at 3:43pm.
Evacuations continued from Lahaina neighborhoods after the Lahaina Bypass was closed.
The US Coast Guard began rescuing residents who jumped into the ocean to escape the flames.
Hawaii National Guard deployed on Maui late at night as fires continued burning out of control.
More than 14,000 Maui customers had lost power by 1:20am as Dora-fuelled winds continued.
Over 2,100 people were reported sheltering overnight at four Maui emergency shelters.
Military helicopters dumped roughly 150,000 gallons of water on active fires during the day.
Drone footage showed fire fronts moving toward Kahului; over 11,000 customers remained without power.
Police chief John Pelletier said it was unclear how many people were unaccounted for across Maui.
California Office of Emergency Services announced deployment of a search-and-rescue team to Maui.
Officials reported the Lahaina fire was 80 percent contained; the town remained without power.
Tourism officials said 14,000 people were moved off Maui with thousands more departing by day's end.
US Coast Guard confirmed rescuing 17 from the water and assisting 40 more who had reached shore.
Maui entered the week under widespread drought, with Hurricane Dora churning south of the islands.
Downed power lines near Lahaina appear to have sparked the first fires in dry grass.
High winds from the distant Dora system rapidly spread flames into Lahaina's historic Front Street.
Maui's cellphone evacuation alert reached only a portion of residents that afternoon.
Governor Josh Green called the event likely the largest natural disaster in Hawaii's history.
At least 53 deaths had been confirmed in Lahaina with many still missing.
President Biden approved a major disaster declaration for Hawaii, unlocking federal aid.
Confirmed dead rose past 80 as search teams entered burned neighbourhoods.
Maui officials said over 2,200 structures had been damaged or destroyed in Lahaina alone.
Hawaiian Electric faced growing scrutiny over whether its power lines ignited the fire.
Officials estimated roughly 1,000 people were still unaccounted for on Maui.
President Biden and First Lady visited Lahaina to meet survivors and emergency workers.
Maui County sued Hawaiian Electric alleging negligence in maintaining its grid ahead of the fire.
Governor Green signed an executive order extending emergency housing for displaced residents.
Confirmed death toll stood at 97 as identification and DNA work continued.
Maui police released an updated unaccounted-for list, narrowing the figure to a handful of people.
